questions.stem: A 19-year-old man is seen after a knee injury. Over the next two days the knee becomes hot, tense, and swollen with a large effusion, and he later develops a deep thigh hematoma. He has had similar deep bleeds before. His platelet count is normal. Which part of hemostasis is most likely defective?
questions.explain: Deep bleeding — hemarthroses and muscle hematomas — is the classic pattern of a secondary hemostasis (coagulation factor) defect such as hemophilia. Mucocutaneous bleeding (petechiae, epistaxis, gum bleeding) would instead point to primary hemostasis (platelets/vWF).

questions.stem: A medical student is asked which screening test reflects the extrinsic coagulation pathway and is used to monitor warfarin therapy. Which test is correct?
questions.explain: The PT/INR reflects the extrinsic and common pathways (factor VII first) and is the standard test for warfarin monitoring. The PTT reflects the intrinsic pathway and is used for heparin and hemophilia.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman is assessed before tonsillectomy. Her PT/INR and PTT are normal and she is cleared for surgery. Postoperatively she has severe bleeding requiring ICU admission, multiple transfusions, and return trips to the OR. On review she reports heavy menstrual bleeding since adolescence and severe postpartum hemorrhage. Which diagnosis best explains her presentation?
questions.explain: Normal PT/PTT does not exclude a bleeding disorder. Lifelong mucocutaneous bleeding — menorrhagia, postpartum hemorrhage, severe surgical mucosal bleeding — strongly suggests vWD, the most common inherited bleeding disorder. Factor XII deficiency prolongs PTT but does not cause bleeding; factor VII deficiency prolongs PT; antithrombin deficiency causes thrombosis.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a new non-blanching petechial rash on both legs. CBC shows platelets 2 ×10⁹/L, hemoglobin 121 g/L, and WBC 5.1 ×10⁹/L. She has no neurologic symptoms. Which additional finding would be most concerning for an increased risk of life-threatening bleeding?
questions.explain: In severe thrombocytopenia, wet purpura — oral mucous-membrane bleeding such as blood blisters — signals higher risk of serious bleeding, including intracranial hemorrhage, compared with petechiae alone. Splenomegaly may explain a cause but does not predict imminent bleeding as strongly.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man in the ICU with E. coli sepsis begins bleeding from IV sites, his nose, and his endotracheal tube. Hemoglobin is 90 g/L, platelets 110 ×10⁹/L, the film shows red-cell fragments, PT/INR and PTT are both markedly prolonged, and fibrinogen is low. What is the most likely cause of his bleeding?
questions.explain: Sepsis plus oozing from lines/tubes, thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT and PTT, low fibrinogen, raised fibrin degradation, and schistocytes is classic DIC. Hemophilia gives isolated PTT prolongation with deep bleeding; ITP gives isolated thrombocytopenia; vWD is mucocutaneous with often-normal screening labs.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man has recurrent hemarthroses and large muscle hematomas. Platelet count and PT/INR are normal, but PTT is prolonged. A mixing study corrects the PTT. Which diagnosis is most likely?
questions.explain: Deep bleeding with a normal PT and prolonged PTT points to an intrinsic-pathway factor (VIII, IX, XI, XII). Correction on mixing indicates a factor deficiency rather than an inhibitor. Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) fits. Warfarin would prolong PT; antiplatelet/Glanzmann defects give mucocutaneous bleeding.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with right calf swelling and tenderness after a long car trip. He is tachycardic at 129, tachypneic at 30, and his oxygen saturation is 90%. Chest X-ray is normal. You strongly suspect PE with DVT. CT pulmonary angiography will be delayed several hours. Platelets, INR, and PTT are normal, and there is no active bleeding. What is the best next step while awaiting imaging?
options: Order D-dimer and wait for the result before any treatment
Reassure and discharge with outpatient follow-up
Insert an IVC filter as first-line therapy
questions.explain: High clinical probability PE/DVT with abnormal vitals and no bleeding contraindication warrants empiric therapeutic anticoagulation while imaging is delayed. D-dimer is not useful when pre-test probability is high; aspirin is inadequate for acute VTE; IVC filters are reserved for patients who cannot be anticoagulated.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man injures his calf playing basketball and now has a sore, swollen calf. He has no prior VTE, no family history, no recent surgery or hospitalization, and no cancer. His clinical probability for DVT is low. Which test is best to rule out DVT?
questions.explain: In a low pre-test probability patient, a negative D-dimer safely rules out DVT. D-dimer should not be used to rule in VTE and is less useful in high-risk or older patients where false positives are common.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman at 26 weeks gestation presents with acute dyspnea and you suspect PE. She also has unilateral leg swelling and tenderness. Which test is the best next step to confirm VTE while minimizing fetal and maternal risk?
options: CT pulmonary angiography as the first and only test
D-dimer as a definitive rule-in test
questions.explain: In pregnancy with suspected PE and leg symptoms, leg Doppler ultrasound is often the preferred first test: finding a DVT establishes the need for anticoagulation (the same treatment as PE) without exposing mother and fetus to chest imaging/contrast. D-dimer cannot rule in VTE and is physiologically elevated in pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman has a tender, hard, erythematous palpable cord running along a superficial vein of the leg. She has no whole-leg swelling, no dyspnea, and no hypoxia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: A tender, hard, palpable cord along a superficial vein is characteristic of superficial thrombophlebitis. DVT more often presents with deep venous tenderness and asymmetric swelling of part or all of the limb.

questions.stem: A patient with confirmed DVT/PE is found two weeks later to have gastric carcinoma after developing melena and a hemoglobin drop from 130 to 80 g/L while on anticoagulation. He is hemodynamically stable after resuscitation, and endoscopy achieves hemostasis. What is the most appropriate anticoagulation strategy after bleeding control?
options: Resume anticoagulation, often using LMWH in active GI malignancy, with close monitoring
Permanently stop all anticoagulation because any bleed contraindicates future treatment
Switch to aspirin because it prevents recurrent PE as effectively as anticoagulation
Place an IVC filter and never restart anticoagulation
Treat for only 24 hours because the clot has already embolized
questions.explain: Cancer-associated VTE carries a high recurrence risk and usually needs extended anticoagulation while cancer is active. Active bleeding may require temporarily holding anticoagulation plus source control and resuscitation; after hemostasis it should be resumed. LMWH is often favored in active cancer, especially with GI malignancy/bleeding. IVC filters are only temporizing when anticoagulation is impossible.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old construction worker is crushed by heavy machinery. He is hypotensive and tachycardic. Primary survey shows a patent airway, good bilateral breath sounds, a soft abdomen, a negative FAST, an unstable pelvis, and bilateral thigh deformity with crepitus. Pulses are present. Which immediate intervention best addresses the likely source of hemorrhagic shock?
options: Pelvic binder application and blood-product resuscitation
Delay resuscitation until CT confirms bleeding
questions.explain: Hypotensive blunt trauma with an unstable pelvis and bilateral femur injuries raises major hemorrhage concern even with a negative FAST, because pelvic/retroperitoneal bleeding may not be detected. Apply a pelvic binder, splint long bones, resuscitate with blood products, and activate the massive hemorrhage protocol.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman receiving unfractionated heparin for a DVT has her platelet count fall from 250 to 95 ×10⁹/L on day 7, and she develops a new clot in the contralateral leg. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Stop all heparin and start a non-heparin anticoagulant
Stop heparin and start warfarin immediately
Reduce the heparin dose and monitor
questions.explain: A >50% platelet fall around day 5–10 of heparin with new thrombosis is classic HIT. Management: stop all heparin and start a non-heparin anticoagulant. Do not start warfarin until platelets recover (risk of venous limb gangrene/skin necrosis), and avoid platelet transfusion, which can worsen thrombosis.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with confusion, a petechial rash, and acute kidney injury. CBC shows thrombocytopenia and the film shows numerous schistocytes. Her PT, PTT, and fibrinogen are all normal. Which is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explain: Thrombocytopenia + schistocytes + neurologic/renal features with normal coagulation studies is TTP, a hematologic emergency treated with urgent plasma exchange. Avoid platelet transfusion (can worsen microthrombi). Normal PT/PTT/fibrinogen argues against DIC, where they would be deranged.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a first unprovoked proximal DVT is hemodynamically stable, has normal renal function, no active bleeding, and reliable follow-up. He has no cancer and is not pregnant. Which initial anticoagulation strategy is most appropriate for outpatient management?
options: An oral DOAC such as apixaban or rivaroxaban
IV unfractionated heparin with PTT monitoring
questions.explain: For a stable, uncomplicated VTE with no contraindication, an oral DOAC is preferred — rapid onset, no routine monitoring, and suitable for outpatient care. UFH is reserved for high bleeding risk/renal failure/anticipated procedures; warfarin requires bridging; aspirin is inadequate; an IVC filter does not treat the clot.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman recovering from hip surgery has a D-dimer ordered as part of a workup. It returns elevated. Which statement best describes how to interpret this result for VTE?
options: It is nonspecific here and cannot confirm VTE; imaging is needed
An elevated value confirms PE and warrants lifelong anticoagulation
A normal value would have been impossible in this patient
It reliably distinguishes DVT from PE
It rules in DIC by itself
questions.explain: D-dimer is sensitive but not specific and is predictably raised by recent surgery, age, inflammation, and hospitalization. In a high-risk/older post-operative patient it cannot rule in VTE — imaging is required. Its value lies in ruling out VTE in low pre-test-probability patients.

questions.stem: A patient on warfarin for a mechanical heart valve needs to understand why the drug works and how it is reversed. Which mechanism and reversal pairing is correct?
options: Inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (↓ II, VII, IX, X); reversed with vitamin K / PCC
Directly inhibits factor Xa; reversed with protamine
Blocks ADP receptors; reversed with platelet transfusion
Activates plasminogen; reversed with tranexamic acid
questions.explain: Warfarin blocks vitamin K epoxide reductase, lowering the vitamin-K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) and proteins C/S; it is reversed with vitamin K and, urgently, PCC. Protamine reverses heparin; idarucizumab reverses dabigatran; TXA is an antifibrinolytic.

questions.stem: During a FAST exam on a trauma patient, the sonographer examines the right upper quadrant. Which space is being assessed for free fluid in this view?
questions.explain: The RUQ FAST view assesses Morison pouch (the hepatorenal recess) for free fluid. The splenorenal space is the LUQ view, the pouch of Douglas/rectovesical pouch is the pelvic view, and the pericardial sac is the subxiphoid view.

questions.stem: A trauma patient with significant hemorrhage receives tranexamic acid early after injury. What is the mechanism by which TXA reduces bleeding?
options: It blocks plasminogen activation, reducing fibrin breakdown
questions.explain: TXA is an antifibrinolytic: it blocks plasminogen binding/activation, so less plasmin is generated and the fibrin clot is preserved. It is an adjunct — not a substitute for source control, resuscitation, or correcting major coagulopathy.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man had a DVT three months ago that was provoked by a major orthopedic surgery, now fully recovered, with no other risk factors. He has completed three months of anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate duration decision?
options: Stop anticoagulation, as 3 months is usually sufficient for VTE provoked by a major transient risk factor
Continue indefinitely because all DVTs require lifelong therapy
Extend to exactly 12 months regardless of provocation
Place an IVC filter for long-term protection
questions.explain: VTE provoked by a major transient risk factor (such as recent surgery) has a low recurrence risk once the factor resolves, so ~3 months of anticoagulation is usually sufficient. Unprovoked or recurrent VTE and active-cancer VTE warrant longer or indefinite therapy.

cards.prompt: Name the three linked stages of hemostasis in order.
cards.answer: Primary hemostasis (vascular response + platelet plug), secondary hemostasis (coagulation cascade → fibrin clot), then fibrinolysis with natural anticoagulant regulation (clot limitation and breakdown).

cards.prompt: How does bleeding pattern localize the defect?
cards.answer: Immediate mucocutaneous bleeding (petechiae, epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia) → platelets/vWF (primary). Delayed deep bleeding (hemarthrosis, muscle/retroperitoneal/intracranial hematoma) → coagulation factors (secondary).

cards.prompt: What are the two roles of von Willebrand factor?
cards.answer: It bridges platelets to exposed subendothelial collagen (via GPIb) for adhesion, and it carries/stabilizes factor VIII in the circulation.

cards.prompt: What is the single most important step in managing DIC?
cards.answer: Treat the underlying cause (e.g. sepsis, trauma, obstetric emergency, malignancy). Product support (platelets, cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen, plasma) is only adjunctive and given for bleeding or planned procedures.

cards.prompt: In HIT, why must warfarin be avoided until platelets recover?
cards.answer: Warfarin acutely lowers proteins C and S, which can precipitate venous limb gangrene and skin necrosis in the prothrombotic HIT state. Use a non-heparin anticoagulant first and add warfarin only after platelet recovery.

cards.prompt: What is the core principle of acute DVT/PE management?
cards.answer: Anticoagulation. If pre-test probability is high and bleeding risk is acceptable, start empiric anticoagulation even before imaging confirms the diagnosis.

cards.prompt: When is an IVC filter indicated, and is it definitive treatment?
cards.answer: Only for acute VTE patients who cannot be anticoagulated. It is not definitive treatment, does not treat existing clot, and should be removed once anticoagulation can resume.

cards.prompt: What does the ATLS primary survey (ABCDE) stand for?
cards.answer: Airway with C-spine protection, Breathing, Circulation with hemorrhage control, Disability (neurologic status), Exposure/environment with hypothermia prevention.

cards.prompt: What class of drug is tranexamic acid and what does it do?
cards.answer: An antifibrinolytic — it blocks plasminogen activation, reducing plasmin-mediated fibrin breakdown so the clot is preserved. Used early in trauma hemorrhage and for mucosal/menstrual bleeding.

cards.prompt: Where does D-dimer come from?
cards.answer: Plasmin (generated when tPA converts plasminogen) degrades crosslinked fibrin; D-dimer is a fibrin degradation product. It is sensitive for clot turnover but nonspecific.

cards.text: The {{PT/INR}} reflects the extrinsic pathway (factor VII) and monitors warfarin, while the {{PTT}} reflects the intrinsic pathway and monitors heparin.
cards.explain: PT → extrinsic/common; PTT → intrinsic/common. This pairing anchors the coagulation grid.

cards.text: Thrombin converts fibrinogen to {{fibrin}}, and factor {{XIII}} crosslinks it into a stable, insoluble clot.
cards.explain: Crosslinking by factor XIII stabilizes the platelet plug; its degradation later yields D-dimer.

cards.text: Spontaneous bleeding risk rises markedly when platelets fall below {{20}} ×10⁹/L, and serious spontaneous bleeding (including intracranial) is a concern below {{10}} ×10⁹/L.
cards.explain: Below 50 carries procedure/trauma risk; below 20 higher spontaneous risk; below 10 serious spontaneous bleeding.

cards.text: Hemophilia A is deficiency of factor {{VIII}}, hemophilia B is deficiency of factor {{IX}}, and both are {{X-linked}} recessive.
cards.explain: Both prolong PTT with normal PT and cause deep bleeding; mixing study corrects unless an inhibitor is present.

cards.text: Heparin is reversed with {{protamine}}, dabigatran with {{idarucizumab}}, and warfarin with {{vitamin K}} (plus PCC urgently).
cards.explain: Knowing the specific reversal agent for each anticoagulant is high-yield for bleeding management.

cards.text: VTE provoked by a major transient risk factor is usually treated for {{3 months}}, whereas recurrent unprovoked VTE is often treated {{indefinitely}}.
cards.explain: Duration balances recurrence risk against bleeding risk and patient preference.

cards.text: Endothelial {{tPA}} converts plasminogen to {{plasmin}}, which breaks down the fibrin clot.
cards.explain: This is the fibrinolytic axis; TXA blocks plasminogen activation to oppose it.

cards.text: D-dimer is best used to {{rule out}} VTE in {{low}} pre-test probability patients; high-probability patients should be {{imaged}} directly.
cards.explain: D-dimer is sensitive but not specific, so it excludes rather than confirms VTE.

cards.prompt: Which receptor mediates the final platelet aggregation step by binding fibrinogen between platelets?
cards.explain: GP IIb/IIIa binds fibrinogen to bridge platelets (aggregation). GP Ib binds vWF for adhesion; P2Y12 and COX-1 are activation targets.

cards.prompt: Which laboratory finding most helps distinguish DIC from other causes of prolonged PT/PTT?
cards.explain: DIC consumes fibrinogen (low) while brisk fibrinolysis raises D-dimer; together with low platelets and schistocytes this is the classic pattern.

cards.prompt: What is the treatment of choice for TTP, and what should be avoided?
cards.explain: TTP is treated urgently with plasma exchange. Platelet transfusion is generally avoided as it may fuel microthrombosis.

cards.prompt: Antiplatelet agents are most relevant to which type of thrombosis?
cards.explain: Arterial thrombi are platelet-rich, so antiplatelets dominate; venous thrombi are fibrin-rich and need anticoagulants.

cards.prompt: Which is NOT a typical differential for unilateral leg swelling mimicking DVT?
cards.explain: Heart-failure edema is typically bilateral. Cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, and superficial thrombophlebitis are classic unilateral mimics.

cards.prompt: What is the main diagnostic imaging test for PE in most adults?
cards.explain: CTPA is the principal test; V/Q is the alternative when CTPA is contraindicated. CXR/troponin/echo support risk stratification, not diagnosis alone.

cards.prompt: Which anticoagulant is often preferred in cancer-associated VTE, especially with GI malignancy/bleeding concerns?
cards.explain: LMWH is frequently favored in active cancer; DOACs may raise GI bleeding concerns in some GI malignancies.

cards.prompt: What is a key adverse effect to monitor with repeated desmopressin dosing?
cards.explain: Desmopressin can cause hyponatremia and fluid retention; avoid repeated dosing without monitoring, and note it is not suitable for all vWD types.

cards.prompt: A negative FAST in hypotensive blunt trauma most importantly fails to exclude bleeding in which location?
cards.explain: FAST is poor at detecting retroperitoneal/pelvic hemorrhage, so a negative FAST does not exclude major bleeding in unstable pelvic injury.

cards.prompt: Which are components of Virchow's triad for VTE risk? (select all)
cards.explain: Virchow's triad = stasis, hypercoagulability, and endothelial injury.

cards.prompt: Which findings are typical of DIC? (select all)
cards.explain: DIC shows low platelets, prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, high D-dimer, and schistocytes.

cards.prompt: Which features raise suspicion for HIT? (select all)
cards.explain: HIT is a clinical-timing diagnosis: large platelet drop, day 5–10 timing, and new thrombosis. Low fibrinogen with bleeding suggests DIC instead.

cards.prompt: Which are red flags requiring urgent evaluation in suspected PE? (select all)
options: Mild pleuritic pain with normal vitals
cards.explain: Hypoxia, tachycardia, and syncope/hypotension flag high-risk PE; isolated mild pleuritic pain with normal vitals is lower acuity.

cards.prompt: Which features increase concern for an inherited bleeding disorder? (select all)
options: A single bleed after major trauma
cards.explain: Lifelong, recurrent, transfusion-requiring, or familial bleeding raises concern; a single bleed after major trauma is lower concern.

cards.prompt: Order the fibrinolysis sequence from first to last:
items: D-dimer is released from crosslinked fibrin
cards.explain: tPA → plasmin → fibrin breakdown → D-dimer release.

cards.prompt: Order the workup of a low-probability suspected DVT:
cards.explain: Low probability → D-dimer → ultrasound if positive → anticoagulate if confirmed.

cards.prompt: Order the immediate response to hypotensive pelvic-crush trauma:
items: Apply pelvic binder and splint femurs
Resuscitate with blood products + activate MHP
cards.explain: Stabilize and control hemorrhage, bind/splint, resuscitate with blood, then definitive control.

cards.prompt: Order the management of a GI bleed in a patient on anticoagulation for cancer-associated VTE:
cards.explain: Stabilize → hold AC → source control → resume AC after hemostasis (untreated VTE remains dangerous).

cards.prompt: Match the coagulation pattern to its likely cause:
pairs: Prolonged PT and PTT with low fibrinogen
cards.explain: The PT/PTT/platelet grid is the backbone of the bleeding workup.

cards.prompt: Match the anticoagulant to its mechanism:
cards.explain: Mechanism dictates monitoring, reversal, and contraindications.

cards.prompt: Match the antiplatelet to its target:
cards.explain: Each acts at a different step of platelet activation/aggregation.

cards.prompt: Match the FAST view to its key space:
cards.explain: Four FAST windows screen for free fluid/blood.

cards.prompt: Match the entity to its discriminating clue:
pairs: Isolated thrombocytopenia, normal coags, no schistocytes
cards.explain: Coagulation studies and the smear separate the three.

cards.prompt: Match the VTE scenario to the preferred agent:
cards.explain: Agent choice is driven by renal function, cancer, pregnancy, and APS.

cards.prompt: How do heparins exert their anticoagulant effect?
cards.answer: They potentiate antithrombin, which then inhibits thrombin (IIa) and factor Xa. Antithrombin deficiency both reduces heparin effect and raises VTE risk.

cards.prompt: What does a mixing study distinguish, and how is it read?
cards.answer: It distinguishes a factor deficiency from an inhibitor when PT/PTT is prolonged: mixing with normal plasma corrects a factor deficiency but fails to correct in the presence of an inhibitor (e.g. factor VIII inhibitor, lupus anticoagulant).

cards.prompt: Besides VTE, which conditions can elevate D-dimer? (select all)
options: A normal young patient at rest
cards.explain: D-dimer rises in cancer, pregnancy, surgery/trauma, infection, age, and DIC — hence its poor specificity.

cards.prompt: Which feature most strongly favors admission rather than outpatient VTE management?
cards.explain: Instability, hypoxemia, RV strain, syncope, high bleeding risk, or unreliable follow-up favor admission; stable low-risk patients can be managed as outpatients.

cards.prompt: Why does an aging population increase both thrombotic and bleeding burden?
cards.answer: VTE risk rises with age (more cancer, immobility, surgery, hospitalization), increasing anticoagulant use; bleeding risk also rises from renal impairment, polypharmacy, falls, frailty, and GI pathology — so treatment must be individualized.

guides.title: Week 15 · 1 Normal Hemostasis & Fibrinolysis
guides.html:
Hemostasis

Normal Hemostasis & Fibrinolysis

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Stop bleeding fast, keep it local: the platelet plug, the fibrin clot, and the brakes that limit both.

Three linked stages

Hemostasis must be rapid enough to prevent exsanguination yet localized enough to avoid pathologic clotting. Thrombosis is clot forming where/when it is not needed.

StageEventTime scale
VasoconstrictionLocal reduction in flowSeconds
PrimaryPlatelet plug (vessel + platelets + vWF)Seconds
SecondaryCoagulation cascade → fibrin clot30–120 s
FibrinolysisClot limitation, breakdown, repairHours–days

Primary hemostasis: the platelet plug

StepKey moleculeDisorder example
Adhesion — platelets stick to subendotheliumvWF bridges collagen to GP IbvWD, Bernard-Soulier
Activation — shape change, granule releaseADP, thromboxane A2, Ca, serotoninAspirin (TXA2), P2Y12 inhibitors (ADP)
Aggregation — platelets bind each otherGP IIb/IIIa binds fibrinogenGlanzmann, GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors
Primary hemostasis defects → mucocutaneous bleeding: petechiae, purpura, epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia, immediate post-procedure bleeding.

Secondary hemostasis: the cascade

PathwayTestFactors
ExtrinsicPT / INRVII
IntrinsicPTT / aPTTXII, XI, IX, VIII
Commonboth PT & PTTX, V, II, fibrinogen

Thrombin (IIa) converts fibrinogen → fibrin; factor XIII crosslinks fibrin into a stable, insoluble clot.

Localizing by bleeding pattern
Immediate mucosal bleeding → platelets/vWF (primary). Delayed deep bleeding — hemarthrosis, muscle/retroperitoneal/intracranial hematoma, re-bleed after surgery → coagulation factors (secondary).

Fibrinolysis & the natural brakes

Endothelial tPA converts plasminogen → plasmin, which degrades fibrin; crosslinked-fibrin breakdown releases D-dimer (sensitive, not specific).

RegulatorActionCorrelation
AntithrombinInhibits thrombin & XaHow heparins work; deficiency → VTE
Protein C + SInactivate Va & VIIIaDeficiency → VTE; warfarin start can be transiently prothrombotic
TFPIInhibits tissue-factor pathwayLimits excess initiation

guides.title: Week 15 · 2 Approach to the Bleeding Patient
guides.html:
Approach

Approach to the Bleeding Patient

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The history is the most powerful test; the screening panel and PT/PTT/platelet grid do the rest.

The bleeding history is the key test

A normal PT/INR and PTT does not exclude a clinically important bleeding disorder — vWD, platelet function defects, mild factor deficiencies, and vascular disorders can all screen normal. Structure the history around:

  • Location — mucocutaneous vs deep.
  • Timing — spontaneous/traumatic, immediate/delayed, acute/chronic/recurrent.
  • Severity — transfusions, iron, surgery, ICU, return to OR.
High concernLower concern
Recurrent spontaneous bleedingSingle bleed after major trauma/surgery
Severe bleeding after minor trauma/proceduresResolved at home without intervention
Menorrhagia with iron-deficiency anemia; PPHNo personal/family history despite prior challenges
Bleeding needing transfusion/OR/ICU; bleeding since childhood; family history

Initial investigations

TestWhy
CBC + blood filmAnemia, platelet count/morphology; schistocytes, blasts
PT/INRExtrinsic/common; warfarin, vitamin K, liver disease, DIC
PTT/aPTTIntrinsic/common; hemophilia, heparin, lupus anticoagulant
FibrinogenLow in DIC, massive bleeding, severe liver disease
vWF antigen/activity + VIIIMucocutaneous bleeding with normal PT/PTT
Mixing studyFactor deficiency (corrects) vs inhibitor (does not)

The PT / PTT / platelet grid

PTPTTPlateletsThink
NNLowITP, marrow disease, sequestration
NNNvWD, platelet function defect, mild factor deficiency
↑NNFactor VII deficiency, early warfarin/vitamin K
N↑NHemophilia A/B, XI/XII, heparin, lupus anticoagulant
↑↑VariableLiver disease, DIC, common-pathway, massive transfusion
Mixing study logic
When PT/PTT is prolonged, a mixing study with normal plasma corrects a factor deficiency but fails to correct an inhibitor (e.g. factor VIII inhibitor, lupus anticoagulant).
Replace what is missing only when clinically indicated — do not reflexively "correct numbers" in the absence of bleeding or a planned procedure.

guides.title: Week 15 · 3 Bleeding Disorders: vWD, Hemophilia, ITP
guides.html:
Disorders

Bleeding Disorders: vWD, Hemophilia, ITP

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The common inherited and acquired causes — how they present, what the labs show, and how they are treated.

von Willebrand disease

The most common inherited bleeding disorder. vWF mediates platelet adhesion and carries/stabilizes factor VIII.

  • Clues: epistaxis, gum bleeding, easy bruising, menorrhagia, PPH, bleeding after dental work/tonsillectomy.
  • Labs: platelets usually normal; PT normal; PTT normal or prolonged if VIII low; confirm with vWF antigen/activity + factor VIII.
SituationTreatment
Mild mucosal bleed / prophylaxisTranexamic acid; desmopressin if responsive type
Major bleed / high-risk surgeryvWF-containing factor concentrate ± TXA
Heavy menstrual bleedingHormonal therapy, TXA, iron replacement
Desmopressin caution
Desmopressin releases stored vWF/VIII but is not appropriate for all vWD types and causes hyponatremia / fluid retention; avoid repeated dosing without monitoring.

Hemophilia A and B

DisorderFactorInheritance
Hemophilia AVIIIX-linked recessive
Hemophilia BIXX-linked recessive

Deep bleeding — hemarthroses, muscle hematomas, delayed surgical bleeding. Labs: normal platelets and PT, prolonged PTT, mixing study corrects (factor deficiency, no inhibitor).

  • Replace deficient factor (VIII or IX concentrate); desmopressin can raise VIII in mild hemophilia A.
  • Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs; treat serious bleeds with factor before imaging delays.
  • Inhibitor development is a major complication that changes management.

Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)

Isolated thrombocytopenia with petechiae/purpura; otherwise normal WBC/Hb unless bleeding.

SeverityManagement
Mild, no bleedingObserve
Significant bleeding / very low countCorticosteroids or IVIG
Life-threatening bleedingPlatelet transfusion (only here — transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed)
Thrombocytopenia differential in 3 buckets: decreased production (marrow failure, leukemia, chemo, alcohol, B12/folate), increased destruction/consumption (ITP, DIC, TTP/HUS, HIT, drugs), sequestration/dilution (splenomegaly, massive transfusion).

guides.title: Week 15 · 4 DIC & the Microangiopathies
guides.html:
Consumption

DIC & the Microangiopathies

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Systemic coagulation activation that consumes platelets and factors — and the look-alikes that share a smear.

Disseminated intravascular coagulation

DIC is systemic coagulation activation with consumption of platelets and clotting factors. It can cause both thrombosis and bleeding.

TriggersTypical findings
Sepsis (esp. gram-negative)Thrombocytopenia
TraumaProlonged PT and PTT
MalignancyElevated D-dimer
Obstetric complicationsLow fibrinogen
Severe inflammation/shockSchistocytes; bleeding from lines, mucosa, tubes, wounds
Management priorities
1. Treat the underlying cause — this is the definitive step.
2. Supportive transfusion only if bleeding/procedure: platelets if low + bleeding; cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate if fibrinogen low; plasma for significant factor deficiency.
3. Avoid reflexively correcting numbers without clinical bleeding.

Distinguishing the low-platelet + schistocyte states

EntityCoagulationSchistocytesDistinctive
ITPNormalNoIsolated thrombocytopenia
TTPNormalYesNeuro/renal ± fever; plasma exchange; avoid platelet transfusion
DIC↑PT/PTT, ↓fibrinogenYesSepsis/trauma/obstetric; high D-dimer
The discriminator
Normal coagulation studies with schistocytes and thrombocytopenia point to TTP/HUS; prolonged PT/PTT with low fibrinogen point to DIC. In pregnancy also consider HELLP and gestational thrombocytopenia.

Why the labs move the way they do

Widespread microthrombi consume platelets and factors faster than they are made → counts fall and PT/PTT prolong. Brisk fibrinolysis of those clots releases D-dimer, and fibrinogen drops as it is consumed. Red cells shredded passing through fibrin strands become schistocytes.

One-line tell: bleeding + thrombosis + sepsis/trauma/cancer + falling platelets + prolonged PT/PTT + low fibrinogen = DIC until proven otherwise.

guides.title: Week 15 · 5 Drugs Affecting Hemostasis
guides.html:
Pharmacology

Drugs Affecting Hemostasis

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Antiplatelets for arterial clot, anticoagulants for venous clot — mechanisms, reversal, and HIT.

Antiplatelet agents (think arterial)

Platelet-rich thrombi dominate in high-flow arterial beds.

DrugMechanismAdverse
AspirinIrreversible COX-1 → ↓thromboxane A2GI bleeding, platelet dysfunction
P2Y12: clopidogrel, ticagrelor, prasugrelBlock ADP-mediated activationBleeding; ticagrelor dyspnea; prasugrel higher bleed risk
GP IIb/IIIa inhibitorsBlock final common aggregationSignificant bleeding, thrombocytopenia

Anticoagulants (think venous)

Venous thrombi are fibrin/red-cell rich and driven by coagulation activation + stasis.

ClassMechanismReversal / notes
UFHPotentiates antithrombin (IIa & Xa)Protamine; rapid on/off; PTT/anti-Xa; useful in severe renal impairment / high bleeding risk
LMWH (enoxaparin)Antithrombin, mainly anti-XaSC, predictable, no routine monitoring; renal dose adjust; favored in cancer-associated VTE
DOAC — Xa (apixaban, rivaroxaban)Direct Xa inhibitionOral, rapid, no INR; preferred for many VTE; avoid in pregnancy
DOAC — IIa (dabigatran)Direct thrombin inhibitionIdarucizumab reversal; renal clearance
Warfarin (VKA)Blocks vitamin K epoxide reductase → ↓II, VII, IX, X, protein C/SVitamin K / PCC; INR monitoring; teratogenic; needed in APS & mechanical valves
Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)
Suspect when platelets fall >50% from baseline, typically 5–10 days after heparin (sooner with recent exposure), or new thrombosis on heparin. Stop all heparin, start a non-heparin anticoagulant, and do not give warfarin until platelets recover (risk of venous limb gangrene/skin necrosis).
Warfarin pitfall: early on it lowers protein C/S faster than the procoagulant factors, creating a transient prothrombotic window — hence bridging for acute VTE and the warfarin-skin-necrosis risk.

guides.title: Week 15 · 6 VTE: Diagnosis of DVT & PE
guides.html:
Thrombosis

VTE: Diagnosis of DVT & PE

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Virchow's triad, the presentations, and the pre-test-probability logic that decides D-dimer vs imaging.

Definitions & Virchow's triad

DVT and PE are manifestations of the same disease; most PEs originate as leg DVTs.

StasisHypercoagulabilityEndothelial injury
Immobility, long travel, surgery, hospitalization, heart failureCancer, pregnancy, estrogen, inherited thrombophilia, APS, ageSurgery, trauma, central catheters
Age is a major prothrombotic state; unprovoked VTE can be the first sign of occult malignancy (gastric, pancreatic, lung, ovarian classics).

DVT presentation

Unilateral leg swelling, calf/thigh tenderness along the deep system, warmth/erythema, pitting edema, calf asymmetry. Differential: cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, muscle tear, superficial thrombophlebitis, venous insufficiency, lymphedema.

PE presentation

Acute dyspnea, tachypnea, tachycardia, hypoxemia, pleuritic chest pain, syncope, hemoptysis, signs of DVT, right-heart strain. Central PE may cause dyspnea/hypoxemia without pleuritic pain (pleura not irritated); peripheral PE with infarction causes pleuritic pain.

Red flags — urgent
O₂ sat ≤90%, HR >100, elevated RR, hypotension/syncope, altered mental status, severe distress → ED-level evaluation, oxygen, imaging, possible empiric anticoagulation.

The pre-test-probability rule

ProbabilityNext step
Low / unlikely (Wells)D-dimer: if negative, VTE ruled out; if positive → image
High / likely (Wells)Image directly (compression ultrasound for DVT; CTPA for PE); do not rely on D-dimer
Pregnancy + leg symptomsLeg Doppler first — finding DVT establishes treatment without chest imaging

D-dimer is sensitive but not specific (raised in cancer, infection, trauma, surgery, pregnancy, age, DIC). Best use: ruling out VTE in low-probability patients. CTPA is the main PE test; V/Q is the alternative when CTPA is contraindicated.

Why not image everyone?
Radiation/contrast risk, limited resources/delays, incidental findings, detection of insignificant subsegmental clots, and unnecessary anticoagulation with its bleeding risk.

guides.title: Week 15 · 7 VTE Management & Anticoagulation
guides.html:
Treatment

VTE Management & Anticoagulation

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Anticoagulate, choose the right agent, set the duration, and navigate the cancer/bleeding tightrope.

Core principle

Acute DVT/PE management = anticoagulation, anticoagulation, anticoagulation. If pre-test probability is high and bleeding risk acceptable, start empiric anticoagulation while awaiting imaging.

Assess bleeding risk first

Active bleeding, recent major surgery/trauma, platelet count, INR/PTT, hemoglobin trend, renal/liver function, history of intracranial hemorrhage.

Choosing the agent

ScenarioPreferred
Stable, uncomplicated VTEDOAC (oral, no monitoring)
Severe renal impairment / procedure likelyUFH (rapid off, reversible)
Cancer-associated, esp. GI malignancy/bleed concernLMWH
PregnancyLMWH
Antiphospholipid syndromeWarfarin (over DOACs)

Admit vs outpatient

AdmitConsider outpatient
Hemodynamic instability; hypoxemia/oxygen needLow-risk PE or DVT
High-risk PE / RV strain; syncopeNormal stable vitals, adequate oxygenation
Active bleeding/high bleeding risk; renal failureLow PESI/sPESI
Unreliable follow-up / social barriersReliable follow-up & medication access

Duration of anticoagulation

VTE typeDuration
Provoked by major transient risk factorUsually 3 months
UnprovokedMinimum 3 months, reassess; often extended
Recurrent unprovokedOften indefinite
Cancer-associatedWhile cancer active/treated and risk persists
Cancer-associated VTE with active bleeding
Stabilize ABCs → temporarily hold anticoagulation if bleeding is significant → resuscitate → control the source (endoscopy/PPI/IR) → resume anticoagulation once hemostasis is achieved (untreated PE/DVT remains dangerous). LMWH often favored.
IVC filters
Not definitive treatment — reserved for acute VTE patients who cannot be anticoagulated. Remove when anticoagulation can resume; filters carry their own thrombosis risk and do not treat existing clot.

guides.title: Week 15 · 8 Trauma, Massive Hemorrhage, FAST & TXA
guides.html:
Trauma

Trauma, Massive Hemorrhage, FAST & TXA

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The hypotensive trauma patient: ATLS, the FAST exam, blood-product resuscitation, TXA, and the aging-population context.

ATLS primary survey

For the hypotensive trauma patient, work the A-B-C-D-E:

  • Airway with C-spine protection
  • Breathing and ventilation
  • Circulation with hemorrhage control
  • Disability (neurologic status)
  • Exposure/environment, prevent hypothermia
Unstable pelvis + bilateral femur injuries + hypotension/tachycardia → suspect major hemorrhage (retroperitoneal/pelvic + long-bone loss), even with a negative FAST.

Early interventions

Large-bore IV/IO access; blood products over large-volume crystalloid; pelvic binder for unstable pelvis; splint femurs; analgesia (ketamine/fentanyl); early TXA; CXR + pelvic X-ray; FAST; activate the massive hemorrhage protocol.

FAST exam

Looks for free fluid (usually blood).

ViewLandmark
RUQMorison pouch (hepatorenal recess)
LUQSplenorenal space + diaphragm
PelvisRectovesical (male) / pouch of Douglas (female)
SubxiphoidPericardial effusion
FAST limitation
A negative FAST does not exclude hemorrhage, especially retroperitoneal/pelvic bleeding. Integrate with hemodynamics, not in isolation.

Tranexamic acid

An antifibrinolytic — blocks plasminogen activation, reducing fibrin breakdown. Uses: early trauma hemorrhage, heavy menstrual bleeding, mucosal bleeding in vWD/platelet disorders, peri-procedural prophylaxis. Not a substitute for source control, resuscitation, or correcting major coagulopathy.

Public health: aging & clot risk

Seniors are a rapidly growing group in Canada. VTE risk rises substantially with age, and older adults more often have cancer, immobility, surgery, and hospitalization — so anticoagulant use grows. Bleeding risk also rises (renal impairment, polypharmacy, falls, frailty, GI pathology).

Clinical takeaway: in older adults, individualize VTE prevention/treatment — balance thrombotic risk, bleeding risk, renal function, goals of care, adherence, and follow-up.

guides.title: Week 15 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Blood II

Week 15 · Blood II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Major hemorrhage / hemorrhagic shock → blood products over crystalloid, pelvic binder, early TXA, activate massive-transfusion protocol
DIC (sepsis/trauma/OB + bleeding, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer) → treat the cause + product support
TTP (schistocytes + low platelets, normal coags ± neuro) → plasma exchange; do NOT transfuse platelets
HIT (platelets ↓>50%, day 5–10, new clot) → stop ALL heparin + non-heparin anticoagulant; no warfarin until platelets recover
High-probability PE + acceptable bleeding risk + imaging delayed → start empiric anticoagulation
Warfarin + life-threatening bleed → vitamin K + PCC (reverse fast)
Severe thrombocytopenia + wet purpura (oral blood blisters) → high serious-bleeding risk

Normal hemostasis Physiology

Primary platelet plug — vWF/GPIb adhesion, GPIIb/IIIa aggregation
Secondary cascade → fibrin; PT = extrinsic (VII), PTT = intrinsic (VIII/IX/XI/XII)
Mucocutaneous bleed primary (platelets/vWF)
Deep / delayed bleed secondary (coagulation factors)
Fibrinolysis tPA → plasmin; D-dimer sensitive, not specific
Natural brakes antithrombin, protein C/S, TFPI

Approach to bleeding Workup

History the key test; normal PT/PTT doesn't exclude (vWD, platelet defect)
PT↑ only factor VII / early warfarin
PTT↑ only hemophilia, heparin, lupus anticoagulant
PT↑ + PTT↑ liver disease, DIC, common pathway
Mixing study corrects = deficiency; no correction = inhibitor

Bleeding disorders Disorders

vWD mucocutaneous; normal/↑PTT; TXA / desmopressin / vWF concentrate
Hemophilia A (VIII) / B (IX) X-linked, deep bleeds, ↑PTT corrects → factor replacement
ITP isolated thrombocytopenia; steroids/IVIG if bleeding; platelets only if life-threatening
Platelet thresholds <50 procedure · <20 spontaneous · <10 serious

DIC & microangiopathies Consumption

DIC ↑PT/PTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer, schistocytes → treat cause + products
TTP schistocytes + neuro/renal, normal coags → plasma exchange
Discriminator ITP (normal coags, no schisto) · TTP (schisto, normal coags) · DIC (↓fibrinogen)

Drugs affecting hemostasis Pharm

Antiplatelets (arterial) aspirin (COX-1); clopidogrel/ticagrelor (P2Y12); GPIIb/IIIa
UFH / LMWH potentiate antithrombin; UFH reversed by protamine
DOAC apixaban/rivaroxaban (Xa); dabigatran (IIa, reversed by idarucizumab)
Warfarin blocks vitamin K epoxide reductase; reverse with vitamin K / PCC
HIT stop heparin → non-heparin agent; no warfarin until platelets recover
Warfarin pitfall protein C/S drop first → transient prothrombotic (bridge)

VTE diagnosis Thrombosis

Virchow's triad stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury
Low probability D-dimer (rules out if negative)
High probability image directly — US (DVT), CTPA (PE)
D-dimer sensitive, not specific (rule out only)
PE red flags O₂ ≤90%, HR >100, syncope, hypotension
Pregnancy + leg sx leg Doppler first

VTE management Treatment

Core anticoagulate; empiric if high-prob + delayed imaging
Agent DOAC (stable) · LMWH (cancer/pregnancy) · UFH (renal/procedure) · warfarin (APS/valve)
Duration provoked 3 mo · unprovoked ≥3 mo (often extended) · recurrent/cancer longer
Cancer VTE + bleed hold → source control → resume (LMWH)
IVC filter only if can't anticoagulate; not definitive

Trauma · FAST · TXA Trauma

ATLS ABCDE; unstable pelvis + femurs = major hemorrhage even if FAST negative
Resuscitate blood products > crystalloid; pelvic binder; splint; early TXA
FAST views RUQ Morison · LUQ splenorenal · pelvis · subxiphoid pericardial
TXA antifibrinolytic (blocks plasminogen activation)
Aging ↑VTE and ↑bleeding risk → individualize
Week 15 · Blood II · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.use: Acute VTE; situations needing rapid on/off or reversal
Acute VTE; favored in cancer-associated VTE and pregnancy

facets.contra: Avoid in pregnancy; caution in renal/hepatic disease

facets.mechanism: Blocks vitamin K epoxide reductase → ↓II, VII, IX, X, protein C/S

facets.use: Rapid warfarin reversal in major bleeding
facets.mechanism: Concentrated factors II, VII, IX, X

facets.use: Trauma hemorrhage (early), menorrhagia, mucosal bleeding in vWD/platelet disorders
facets.monitoring: Not a substitute for source control/resuscitation

facets.use: Mild vWD / mild hemophilia A; uremic bleeding
facets.mechanism: Releases stored vWF and factor VIII
facets.contra: Not for all vWD types; avoid repeated dosing without monitoring
